posts.author: Avin
posts.community: KRN Heat Exchanger and Refrigeration Ltd
posts.text: Say we suppose 7x of current sales that is 4200 crore. For sanity purpose let’s say 4000 crore in FY 2028 from the current 600 crore. Now come to OPM - say all government incentives etc etc take it to 22%. For every rupee sales increase they need to invest working capital and capex also post FY 2028. Even before that if they want to grow in 2028 due to capacity caps. FY 2028 EBITDA comes at 880 crore. PAT ~ 600 crore and free cash at ~ 400 crore. Main problem is they need to further keep investing and growing for the next 7-8 years at 20% rev growth rate to justify a ~ 8000 crore mcap in FY 28…

posts.author: Mohit_Jariwala
posts.community: KRN Heat Exchanger and Refrigeration Ltd
posts.text: So it looks like instead of manufacturing everything in-house using copper/aluminum, they may have: bought more ready-made components/products from vendors and directly sold them. That shifts the cost from: “Raw material consumed” → to → “Stock-in-trade purchases”. Still, your observation is valid because copper/aluminum prices were high, and inventory wasn’t large enough to fully explain this shift. Worth tracking: what exactly they traded, whether outsourcing has increased, and if this becomes a permanent trend.

posts.author: vivek_lakhani
posts.community: KRN Heat Exchanger and Refrigeration Ltd
posts.text: image 1226×1325 246 KB Any idea, how come on such incremental revenues, their RAW material consumed is so less compared to historically as percentage of sales ? Aluminum & copper prices, their key RAW materials were already trading at peak. even they didnt have that big left over inventory from previous quarter to justify such high revenue over such low cost of material consumed ? and what products this company typically trades , where their stock-in trade jumped nearly 4X from previous quarter ?
